comments.content:
[IMAGE:0]It's getting on for midnight, so we can proceed with a new Magic Monday. Ask me anything about occultism and I'll do my best to answer it. Any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. (Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here.

The image?  That's the sixteenth card in The Sacred Geometry Oracle. Card 16, The Octahedron, when upright tells you that your best option involves mediating or balancing between different forces; when reversed, it warns you that you're trying to do too much and risking dispersing your energy. The waves on the sides of the image tell you that this card belongs to the second third of the oracle, which corresponds to Gwyar, the principle of fluidity and change. 

[IMAGE:1]

I've had several people ask about tipping me for answers here, and though I certainly don't require that I won't turn it down. You can use the button above to access my online tip jar. If you're interested in political and economic astrology, or simply prefer to use a subscription service to support your favorite authors, you can find my Patreon page here and my SubscribeStar page here. 
 
[IMAGE:2]I've also had quite a few people over the years ask me where they should buy my books, and here's the answer. Bookshop.org is an alternative online bookstore that supports local bookstores and authors, which a certain other gargantuan corporation doesn't, and I now have a shop there, which you can check out here. Please consider patronizing it if you'd like to purchase any of my books online.

And don't forget to look up your Pangalactic New Age Soul Signature at CosmicOom.com.

With that said, have at it!

***This Magic Monday is now closed. See you next week!***

1) Funny. And the cube is therefore the indicator of wandering monsters...

2) You have a lot of eminent dead people to choose among, since a great deal of fine work in the earth sciences was done in the 20th century by people who have since died. Don't miss Stephen Jay Gould, for example! If you haven't yet read Charles Darwin's The Origin of Species that should be high up on your list. As for specific books on the atmosphere, on the one hand, and the Gulf and your local rivers on the other, here again, dead writers ought to be very easy to find. replies.title: atmospheric reading
replies.content:
Hadley's classic paper on atmospheric dynamics, "Concerning the cause of the general trade winds", published in 1735, is available in PDF form from the University of Exeter here:

https://empslocal.ex.ac.uk/people/staff/gv219/classics.d/atmospheric.html

Hadley's explanations of how Earth's rotation produces the patterns of wind in the atmosphere is still taught in every basic fluid and atmospheric dynamics class. Highly recommended.

-Ms. Krieger

https://archive.org/details/physicalgeograp13maurgoog

SamChevre

Texas Titles:
Legends and Lore of Texas Wildflowers by Elizabeth Silverthorne (Interesting and informative stories of various Texas flowers, includes medicinal lore.)

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Okay, a planet is well-dignified when it has certain factors that make it stronger and more positive -- for example, when it's in certain signs where it's naturally strong, or when another planet is in a favorable aspect to it. A planet is ill-dignified when it has factors that make it weaker and less positive -- for example, when it's in certain signs where it's naturally weak, or when another planet is i9n unfavotable aspect to it. An astrological election is a time and date you choose to do something for astrological reasons -- for example, if I'm going to submit a manuscript to a publisher, I might elect a time and date when the positions of the planets favor such an action.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you. What about cazimi? Is it anything like calzone? 😄

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Nope. "Cazimi" just means that a planet is in very close conjunction with the Sun. Most of the time, when a planet is conjunct the sun it's called "combust," meaning that it's overwhelmed by the force of the Sun and loses much of its power, but when a planet is cazimi -- within 17 minutes of arc of the Sun's position -- it picks up strength again. So it's a brief window of strength in the middle of the conjunction period.

Hmm -- I suppose you could think of the combust period as the crust and the cazimi interval as the filling...

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) Start by getting your natal chart and then reading a bunch of introductory books that claim to explain your natal chart. Compare what they have to say with what you know about yourself. If there are people close to you who give their permission for this, do the same thing with their natal charts. You can also find the natal charts of public figures of past and present -- there are huge archives of these online -- and analyze them. That'll give you a good foundation. Then decide what other kinds of astrology you want to learn, get some books on it, and get crackin'.

2) What kind of magic you should pursue isn't something the stars decide for you. It's something you decide for yourself. Pick something that appeals to you and work on it systematically for a year and a day, then decide whether you want to continue it. Will and imagination are paramount in magic, so start exercising them from the start!

replies.title: Re: Kilner
replies.content:
I've been following the discussion on the Kilner goggles on 4chan for a while. There was an Anon using ZWB1 filters from China because they had the same bandpass signature as the compound Kilner used. He achieved middling success, and it sounded like his results could be achieved just sitting in a dark room like Baron Von Reichenbach. A chemist chimed in and mentioned that the compound in Kilner goggles actually fluoresces certain invisible wavelengths, thus shifting invisible frequencies closer to the range of human perception, so a pure bandpass filter may not achieve the same results. Another Anon chimed in over the months talking about how he had access to some papers and correspondences from Kilner's estate as well as an original model of the Kilner goggles. He claimed that Kilner had switched to using a combination of blue and "traffic light green" filters towards the end of his career, and that the goggles made with the original compound have an obvious and strong effect when first looking through them. He also posted some photos through a selection of his goggles, and you can clearly see an energetic aura around his hand in the ones that are all blue. The Anon with access to some of Kilner's papers claimed that making your own goggles with multiple coloured filters was possible however and claimed success himself. There was also talk of an alternate compound called pinacyanol which is extremely similar to diacyanin (actually called Diacyanine-A) and the Anon posted a picture with a bottle of it next to his collection of the various aura goggles and lenses he's collected or built, so perhaps that's how he built the working blue prototypes.

Here's a link with the most illuminating posts and pictures, although they're buried in a lot of antisemitism and meaningless drivel (as is common on 4chan):
https://archive.4plebs.org/x/thread/28213331/

I think more important work would be to identify other substances to get the same effect

Greetings JMG,

Some questions that came up this week:
1. As I read the mention of ukranian witches doing a ritual against Putin I asked myself, what do sane occultists do in a situation like this? In the last weeks you appeared on a podcast deliniating a preaty bad astrological chart for this war, and the last mention of Mundi Sadhu on this blog implies, that he did not sit out the war in his native Poland. How would you handle finding yourself in a country on the brink of war?

2. Last week I got indirectly asked about what a mage can do to call rain? The man had no idea about me, and I joked about rain dancing. Since we are going trough a dry spell here. It bodes a serious question: what can a druid do to bring rain?

Best regards,
V

replies.title: Re: War mages and rain callers
replies.content:
1) Dion Fortune's a good example, because she did magic straight through the Second World War with good effect. Her rule was precise: use magic to strengthen what you want to support, never to tear down what you want to oppose. If you want to support your country you can do workings to strengthen the national egregor. Fortune's book The Magical Battle of Britain is a good resource.

2) It depends on the cause of the drought. Sometimes it's a temporary glitch in the balance of forces, and can be balanced out to bring rain. Sometimes it's part of a bigger balancing process in the atmosphere, and then there's not much you can do. Divination will tell you which is which; if it's something you can influence, you can call on air and water spirits, and also on thunder deities, to do what they do best and make it rain.

comments.title: Translation update
comments.content:
Publishing delays at the moment for Etteilla's Book of Thoth, but I am enjoying translating Carl du Pref's Das Rätsel des Menschen [The Puzzle of Humanity]. You may be interested in this quote he gives from Kant:
"Life consists in the commerce of the soul with the body; the beginning of life is the beginning of the commerce, the end of life is the end of the commerce. The beginning of the commerce is the birth, the end of the commerce is the death. The duration of the commerce is the life. The beginning of life is the birth; this is, however, not the beginning of the life of the soul, but rather that of the human. The end of life is the death; this is, however, not the end of the life of the soul, but rather of the human. Birth, life, and death are thus only conditions of the soul. […] Therefore the substance remains even though the body passes away, and thus the substance must also have been there when the body arose. […] Life with the human is twofold — the animal and the spirit life. The animal life is the life of the human as human; and with this the body is necessary so that the human lives. The other life is the spirit life where the soul, independent of the body, must continue to exercise the same acts of life."
According to du Prel, "Kant was, however, also clear over it being the occult sciences on which the theory of souls had to be erected."

replies.title: Re: Translation update
replies.content: Thanks for this. That's a fascinating bit of Kant -- did du Pref mention which of Kant's works it's from?

replies.title: Re: Translation update
replies.content:
Immanuel Kant, Vorlesungen über Psychologie: Mit einer Einleitung: Kants mystische Weltanschauung, ed. Carl du Prel (Leipzig: Ernst Günther, 1889), 75, 76, 79.
[Title = Lectures over Psychology, with an Introduction: Kant's Mystical World View (by du Prel)] du Prel states in the work I'm translating that he had the lectures published because they had been excluded from every complete works he knew of.

This edition can be found online at https://books.google.co.nz/books?id=JU0JAQAAMAAJ&printsec=frontcover&source=gbs_ge_summary_r&cad=0#v=onepage&q&f=false
I haven't gone down the rabbit-hole of locating matching English editions of Kant as yet.

He also references another work of Kant's and comments about it:
"Now there are admittedly people who say Kant’s “Träume eines Geistersehers” [Dreams of a Visionary] is only a satire on Swedenborg and the belief in spirits. This view, however, is completely contradicted by the fact that Kant 22 years later held lectures in which he expressed entirely the same views and indeed again in connection to Swedenborg whose ideas he called “sublime”."
That last sentence ended with a footnote to:
Immanuel Kant, Immanuel Kant’s Vorlesungen über die Metaphysik: nebst einer Einleitung, welche eine kurze Uebersicht der wichtigsten Veränderungen der Metaphysik seit Kant enthält, ed. Karl Heinrich Ludwig Pölitz (Erfurt: Keysersche Buchhandlung, 1821).

replies.title: Re: Translation update
replies.content: Thanks for this! I can certainly chase down the English version, as my German is still fairly shaky.

replies.title: It depends on what are the legal options at your place of residence
replies.content:
#2. Be very careful about this. If you were to practice some form of energy healing (e.g. reiki) the authorities cannot accuse you of harming the patient if their official position is that what you do does not exist. As long as you do not present yourself as a medical doctor and make sure to advice your patient to not abandon the official medicine treatment (and keep documentary proof that you unambiguosly did so), you should be out of the hook for any malpractice claim (at least until the bozos in charge decide to make praying illegal).

Herbal remedies, on the other hand, are physical substances with thoroughly documented adverse effects. Maximum dosage is tricky because you cannot precisely measure the exact amount of the active principle present in each individual sample of the species. Also, patients have the mistaken beliefs that 1) because herbs are "natural" they cannot be harmful, and 2) if a little bit is good, more should always be better. They will go, overdose themselves, and then blame you for it (or their doctors will talk them into blaming you, it does not matter at the end). Many practitioners will low-ball the dose in order to avoid this problem, but in this case the treatment will not be as effective as it could have been and that will hit you reputation wise.

If you go this route, at least in the industrialized postmodern world of today, long hours of study is a must. Do not limit yourself to the lore that brought our attention to the remedy. Learn biochemistry, learn physiology and pathology, learn pharmacology. If an accreditation program is available in your jurisdiction, be sure to take it. They may take some of your money but will point you to a fixed study program that will cover everything I have mentioned here and more!

replies.title: Herbalism
replies.content:
Hi Anon--

I think herbal medicine is a worthwhile skill set to have as the legacy medical system goes to heck in a handbasket. Certainly something you can barter for other products or services as needed, although becoming a competent practitioner takes quite awhile (like becoming a mage). Start today! If you are interested in a more formal grounding for your studies, I would suggest considering a master's in herbal medicine. I have 1/2 a master's in herbal medicine from MUIH (Maryland University of Integrated Health) although there are others. It helps force the discipline of learning about the materia medica, phytochemistry, and formulation/medicine making among other things.

If you are interested in herbal medicine meets astrology, I VERY HIGHLY recommend Sajah Popham's school Evolutionary Herbalism. He wrote a book of the same name, so you can read that and see if it resonates for you. [I was quite interested in JMG's recent comment that Nicholas Culpepper, a noted herbalist was also a "crackerjack" astrologer-- I didn't know that and now want to learn more about this].

If you are interested in herbal medicine for disaster relief, I VERY HIGHLY recommend 7 Song's school https://7song.com/. 7 Song is a wonderful teacher and very accomplished with many kinds of herbal medicine but specializes in that used for acute injuries/conditions.

Best wishes on your studies. It's a phenomenally interesting and complex field of endeavor. I am looking forward to retiring [eventually] so I can spend more time on herbal medicine, astrology and magic, all of which take a lot of disciplined study to master.

MJ from MA

replies.content:
1. If you are interested in TCM and Ayurveda, you may also benefit from looking into Thai Yoga. Thai Yoga encompasses Thai bodywork (which is roughly like assisted, prone yoga poses with acupressure along meridian lines) and elements of both TCM (meridians and herbal remedies) and Ayurveda (diagnosis, herbalism, and oil work.)

2. You are asking the question in this forum, so I'm going to recommend looking into Sajah Popham. He is a capable traditional herbalist, and runs the School of Evolutionary Herbalism, which teaches herbalism from a vitalistic perspective, encouraging physical and spiritual study of both the herbs and your patients. His program and books also make significant use of planetary astrology, and alchemical principals.

Mathew wood is a prolific, respected, and highly-readable author, with multiple herbalism books to choose from.

You can also check out Magi Flint and Earthsong Herbals. She is a major presence in the herbalism world, referenced and respected by both the above authors.

3. I echo JMG's suggestion to check your local and national laws before practicing either of the above arts. Often, people in my area end up being certified in a tangential medical field and using their knowledge as a complementary alternative therapy (i.e. a Thai yoga practitioner also being a licensed massage therapist.) Alternately, there are ways to practice these arts solely within a religious context; both are well-suited to this.

replies.title: Re: meditations
replies.content:
Of course there's hope for you. Every student of magic I've ever met had at least one serious weakness in terms of the abilities needed for magical practice. With me it was visualization -- I couldn't picture things in my mind at all, and it took me quite a bit of hard work to get to the point of being able to do so.

Your problem is especially common these days because people in modern industrial countries are systematically taught not to think symbolically and metaphorically. I encourage you to consider adding a simple practice: get a good collection of myths or fairy tales -- Andrew Lang's The Blue Fairy Book and its twenty-odd sequels are good options here -- and read one of them a day. Notice the emotions that come up when you do this. You may find it revealing!

replies.title: Re: meditations
replies.content:
> With me it was visualization -- I couldn't picture things in my mind at all, and it took me quite a bit of hard work to get to the point of being able to do so.

Do you have any references I can explore about how to do this?

I have absolutely zero ability to visualise anything (or any sense actually, no sound, no touch, no emotion, no nothing). I have tried researching everything I can find and the only technique I cam across to address this was image streaming, but I did not see any change from doing the exercises for several months. Most people who report success with that method seem to have started with 'some' ability, even if it was almost imperceptible.

I have spent a long time meditating on the issue, and have a strong sense that the images are there, but that the conscious link is 'missing' for lack of a better word. Maybe it is just a flaw in my neurology in this lifetime, but the side effect is I effectively have no 'memory' in that I cannot recall (or replay) anything from my past - my memories are purely semantic knowledge.

I also wonder if I may have asked this before in the past, but as above, my is memory lacking. So apologies if you have already already answered this :)

replies.title: Re: meditations
replies.content:
Once each day, sit down and imagine something. Do it with your eyes open, so you don't get caught in the "but I don't see it!" trap. Imagine that the thing is in front of you. If you like, imagine what it would be like if you could see it. Let's say you're imagining a ball on the coffee table in front of you. Pay attention to the table; notice which parts of the table you wouldn't be able to see if the ball were there. Keep the semantic dimension out of it as far as you can.

You know what a ball looks like -- if you have trouble with this, get one, set it on the table, and study it. Consider learning to draw -- more than half of learning to draw is learning to pay attention to what you're actually seeing. Also, start paying very close visual attention to the world around you. A lot of people who have trouble visualizing pay very little attention to visual experience, and can change the former by paying more attention to the latter.

Keep doing this for years. That's what I did. I added in other exercises as things began to improve -- imagining myself walking familiar routes, and making myself remember the buildings and other things that surrounded me, is especially useful -- but the key is persistence and attention.

replies.title: Re: meditations
replies.content: Your advice there reminds me of an old exercise from decades back: "If you did know the answer, what would it be?" The first thing off the top of your head is probably it. "If you could see it, what would it look like?" sounds like a good companion to this.

replies.title: Re: meditations
replies.content: Exactly. We know more than we realize, and we can do more than we think.

comments.title: Tarot reversals
comments.content:
Good Morning Mr. Greer-
I have taken action on the widespread suggestion to obtain a separate Tarot deck for divination (versus for readings), and am wondering how, with a special and consecrated deck, you would advise introducing reversed cards into the deck. Does one intentionally shuffle them in? Does one wait as, over time, cards are dropped on the floor or mis-shuffled? If there is an advisement on how to do this, or how this has traditionally been done, I would like to follow that.
With gratitude,
Matthew

replies.title: Re: Tarot reversals
replies.content:
No, you simply change the way you shuffle the cards. When you divide the deck before shuffling, turn one-half of the deck end-for-end, so that the end that was up is now down, then shuffle the cards together. Half the deck is now reversed. Repeat the process every single time you shuffle and you'll have a deck thoroughly mixed between upright and reversed.

I habitually take the top half of the deck in my left hand and turn it end-for-end, while taking the bottom half in my right hand and leaving it unrotated. Choose some such gimmick and you'll have plenty of inverted cards to read.

replies.title: Re: Tarot reversals
replies.content: can you explain the difference between divination with tarot/oracle vs a reading?

replies.title: Re: Tarot reversals
replies.content: Um, I'm not aware of a difference. Divinations are readings and readings are divinations.

replies.title: Re: Charisma
replies.content: There's a difference between the kind of artificial charisma I was discussing and what I suppose you can call natural charm. Some people are charming because they're genuinely interesting, attractive, and pleasant -- they don't have anything to worry about. Others go to various expedients to manufacture charisma, or have it manufactured for them by fans or the media. They're at very high risk. Of course there are plenty of mixed cases in between. The best way to handle being involved in an art or other practice that requires drawing the attention of the public is to work at being a genuinely interesting, attractive, and pleasant person; do that and you run no risks at all.

I was comparing the CGD conjuration material with the equivalent sections in Circles of Power and noted that Circles of Power seems to approach conjuration with a hazmat suit on with protective evocation circles, notes of which incense scrubs away unruly spirits, a full exorcism ritual, and section on obsession, while the CGD conjuration material mentions nothing of dangers or defenses at all.

replies.title: Re: Conjuration
replies.content:
When I wrote Circles of Power I'd been practicing magic for twenty years and the only form I'd ever experienced was Golden Dawn magic, which comes with the hazmat approach wired in. It was later on that I began studying other magical traditions and found that, while prudence and the ability to use decisive force are always essential in evocation, ordinary banishings and protective workings are quite adequate.

As for the risks, it really does matter what kind of spirits you work with. Angels and intelligences are by and large very safe to work with, and elementals of all classes are safe, too, if you make contact with the divine powers of the elements first and work with and through them. Also, of course, a friendly and courteous attitude does wonders. The monotheist magical traditions (with the pleasant exception of the Rosicrucians) have a habit of bullying and browbeating spirits -- "I command ye by the Holy Names, and bind ye to perfect obedience!" -- which is guaranteed to make them irritable. A less arrogant approach really does get better results.

comments.title: Hostility towards Boomers
comments.content:
[JMG, I submitted this to a non-MM thread last night thinking I was on MM. Maybe you could post it here?]

Regarding intergenerational hostility towards the Boomers: I’m near the end of the boomers, born in 1958. My daughters were born in 2003 and 2010, Mom in ‘68. One thing I’ve persistently noted is how difficult it has been for my daughters and I to relate to each other, certainly after they steeped themselves in media, and became involved with peers. (Neither Mom nor I watched TV, but inlaws pressured Mom to get one, and it did serve as a babysitter so Mom could study while I worked)

Our younger daughter has been explicitly critical of Mom and me for having her “so late” in our lives. “You and Mom are so old you just don’t understand anything!” I’ve long been somewhat amused by that stance, but I’ve also been aware aware that the dominant culture is hostile to most parents’ values. For example, when I was growing up, homosexuality/lesbianism simply wasn’t discussed around children. That sort of sexual activity was generally regarded as immoral by the public, and I recall liberals regarding it with a grudging tolerance, as in “It really is nobody’s business but their own, it just shouldn’t be paraded in other people’s faces”. Or, say, for a man to dress in women’s clothes in public was a crime; as at best it was a kind of public indecency like flashing, and at worst a stratagem to garner opportunities to rape.

A few months ago, I took my 11-year old daughter to a bookstore, and I was going to vet her selections before buying them. We were in the “Teen” section. The first book she showed me opened with a paragraph about a 14 year-old girl with her 16 year-old brother who was driving their mother’s car. Their mom was in the back seat, passed out drunk. “No” I said, “we’re not going to get this”. The next book (still in the Teen section) was about a 12 year-old girl being “groomed” by an older man for the “sex trade”. “I’m sorry, we can’t get this one either”. The next book was about two teenage girls, who had had a lesbian affair over the preceding summer, and how they would get along during the school year. I put the kabosh on that too. My daughter knew something of the plot beforehand, and she exploded. “Why not?” Me: “I just don’t think it’s appropriate for an 11-year old. Frankly nothing I’ve seen in this section is”. “You just don’t like it because it’s about a same-sex relationship. Let’s go home!”

Any of those plots would have been regarded as obscene by any parent when I was 11. And yet here they are, so mainstream (this was a Barnes and Noble Bookstore), that as far as I could tell, the entire contemporary teen genre was like that. I offered to take her to the “Classics” section, but daughter said “No! That’s boring!”.

Reading over this thread just now, it hit me: My daughters and I are from different, conflictual cultures. Like the children of immigrant families, they’re jettisoning their parents’ ways and adopting the ways of the culture they are immersed in. And let me tell you, there is no kindness or regard for anyone in my age group, least of all a man. I never in my worst nightmares envisioned that being a parent would be like this.

JMG, ‘ got any occult tips to help this situation? I’m empty handed.

—Lunar Apprentice

replies.title: Re: Hostility towards Boomers
replies.content:
Lunar Apprentice--

Not JMG and this isn't (directly) occult-related. And it's me offering advice unsolicited, so feel free to ignore. I certainly intend no offense.

I'm not a Boomer, being an Xer (born in the early 70s), but my relationship with my daughter has encountered similar cultural differences (she was born right around the turn of the century) and as we've gone through the years, I've often noted the cultural contrast in our "natural" ways of thinking. (The concept of gender fluidity is something I simply cannot grasp, for example.)

My daughter came out when she was fifteen. There was some initial pushback from some of the family ("Are you sure? Maybe it's just a phase?") but she was quite adamant. For me, it offered a lot of clarity ("So that's why she never went through a 'boy crazy' period...") as well as some relief that I wasn't going to have to worry about the intentions of hormone-swamped boys (remembering that time of my own life all too well). In some of our subsequent conversations, she revealed to me that her "proclamation" was the culmination of a good amount of self-examination and reflection, and certainly not a fad or a whim.

One thing that has worked for me, on the topic of sexuality as well as others, is to keep an open mind and to keep the conversation respectful. Now, my daughter wasn't eleven when we were discussing this stuff, and there's a difference between eleven and fifteen, but kids these days comprehend more than we realize and I think it is important to acknowledge that. This isn't to say that a parent should agree (or give permission for something that one thinks is harmful), but it *is* important that parental disagreement be communicated in a way that treats the child with a degree of respect, if only to keep the door open for future conversations. If a child thinks a parent isn't going to listen, then communication will potentially dry up at a time when it is most needed.

I've had to have any number conversations well outside my comfort zone as my daughter grew up, but partly because of them, we were able to talk pretty openly through those formative years and have a good rapport today.

Just my own perspective, for what it's worth.

I wish you luck!

--David BTL

replies.title: Re: Hostility towards Boomers
replies.content:
Hostility toward parents is simply a natural part of many children's growing up process. (Not for all children by a long shot, but for many.) Part of being a good parent is being able to absorb hard blows from your children without holding it against them or striking back.

And yes, parenting is truly one of the very hardest things any adult will ever have to do.

As for that visit to the bookstore, ask yourself honestly--were you also trying to protect yourself from having to face the fact that your 11-year old daughter has almost certainly started to think hard about relationships and also about various kinds of sex, straight and otherwise, kinky as well as vanilla. That's just a consequence of the natural hormonal changes that preceed obvious outward signs of puberty by a few years, that is, as early as the fifth or sixth grade (10-12 years old). Books are one way they can get help safely in dealing with all these confusing new feelings and interests. Children are not these pure-minded little humans; they are little animals with human potential, and sometime around 10 or 11 they become lusty little animals in the privacy of their inner selves. That can be hard for a parent to accept, and especially hard for a father to accept in his daughters.

Would it have been better for her, even if very very much harder for you as her father, to let your daughter get and read books like the ones she wants to, and then be open to talking frankly and deeply with her about the problems they deal with and the questions they raise? You can't isolate her from her peer-culture. All any parent can ever do is to help their children cope with its demands in a way that aids them in navigating its perils under their own power.

replies.title: Re: Looking for prayer suggestions for a healthy pregnancy
replies.content:
First off, congratulations and good luck!

In my rather limited experience, I've gotten the impression that different Goddesses have different roles for different parts of the process (with some overlap), and praying to them at those different parts has seemed to go well for me and my wife.

By way of an example to illustrate what I mean, in the Norse/Germanic pantheon, here's my take on which Goddesses might be good to pray to throughout:
- Freyja: for an enjoyable, loving sexual relationship with your spouse (the God Freyr might also be a good choice here, especially for a lady)

Jeff

replies.title: Re: Olden texts on sacred geometry
replies.content: The 18th and 19th centuries were the dark ages of sacred geometry, the period in which it was rarely studied at all, and those who did study it approached it purely as a matter of symbolism. George Winslow Plummer's Rosicrucian Symbology is a better example than most. comments.content:
Good afternoon JMG and Commentariat,

1. Any recommendations for developing the SOP (I practiced it for a few months a few years back,) while continuing my daily work with the Heathen LBRP and the MP? I wish to continue my work with the Heathen GD for the year and day (and likely more,) but also desire to restart and revitalize my earlier work with the Druid Magic Handbook within the new framework I'm developing as my study and practice progresses.

I experience the vibrating of words and names as if I've rung a bell. I see the runes or pentacles I've drawn as light emitting, crystalline solids that vibrate and ring like crystal singing bowls or wine glasses when I vibrate words and names during the LBRP and MP.

3. Years ago I hypothesized that religions were all just different languages pointing to the same spiritual reality, and I attempted to learn as many of those languages as I could in order to speak with believers of those faiths in their own terms and better understand their perspectives. Over time, I felt that that belief was false and that both language and religion are more important than I made them out to be by conflating them. The catalyst for that change was feeling changes within me as I used different words for the same basic internal understanding. I found that the words I used were losing meaning to me and it felt like that was watering down my connection with my actual beliefs and spiritual experiences.

I’ve since come into the habit of using my own terms for my beliefs and generally keeping them to myself, while acknowledging others' beliefs and their language to describe them as not only unique in perspective but also as pointing to unique spiritual realities. There is a classic analogy of different religions experiencing God, being like three blind men touching different parts of a single elephant and arguing that it’s three different animals. I used this analogy in the past to build bridges between myself and people practicing other religions; but I now feel it's disingenuous. It seems to me that all the work of trying to find parallels and common ground is less useful than finding mutual respect and social/cultural tools for coexisting.

3.1 First, I'm curious on others' feelings on this. JMG (and commentariat,) do y'all believe that much of spiritual and religious terminology and names for beings actually describe the same base spiritual "things," or do you feel that different terms lead to different "things." I'm always open to a third option. (Ooooh, that's a new affirmation!)

3.2 JMG, you've mentioned the Masonic requirement of belief in a supreme being. How do you reconcile, balance, and/or integrate a supreme being into polytheism? Is it a matter of linguistics or of your perceived spiritual reality?

Thank you JMG for this space and for your comments.
Thank you everyone else for your thought provoking questions, and thoughtful commentary!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I don't recommend combining the SoP and the LBRP in any way; they work with different basic energy patterns. Choose one or the other and concentrate on that.

2) Focus on the physical for now. The inner dimensions will ripen in their own time.

3.1) That's an exceedingly complicated issue! The Neoplatonists, whose ideas I generally follow, had it that the gods in themselves (on the noetic plane) were distinct from the gods as they are imagined by human worshippers (on the psychic plane), but that the images and names we've got are as high as we can reliably reach and it's best to stick with those. Thus it may well be that Thor, Perun, Shango, Sucellos, and all the other hot-tempered thunder gods associated with great appetites and the color red are all the same noetic entity, on its own plane, but the forms taken by that noetic entity on the plane we can reach are different, and so you're better off treating them as different beings. Of course that implies that the noetic entity we're discussing is entirely separate from the entity who is the original of Freya, Aphrodite, Erzulie, and all the other love and fertility goddesses; each noetic entity is an reflection on the noetic plane of some aspect of the One, the impersonal transcendent unity on the henadic plane, but then so is every other noetic entity including your higher self and mine...

3.2) Most polytheist faiths teach that one of the gods is supreme over the others. It's always seemed to me that "supreme being" is a good label for such an entity. When I became a Mason, nobody asked me whether I believed in only one deity -- just whether I believed in a supreme being and an afterlife. That I also believe in lots of other gods and goddesses, and in a form of afterlife that's not part of the Abrahamic faiths, never came into the conversation.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
3.1. Coca-Cola and Pepsi are arguably both manifestations of the same "bitter-sweetened-caffeinated-fizzy-water" product niche, but they are manifestations of different "consumer intertemporal motivational variation" preference niches (reputedly Coke is less enjoyable than Pepsi at first but takes longer to stop enjoying in one sitting), and different "locations for a bottling plant" and "specific random accidents of advertising image and product development" niches, and the same "general bottle shape and material" engineering niches, and so on. Some things go together, and some things don't, and some things change how other things manifest. And sometimes there are many many things packaged together that are independent enough that they are each substitutible individually, but all hang together in a diffuse way, so that you shouldn't change many of them without a lot of... broad insight? chokhmah? about the whole thing.

It would take a while, but if you have the time, the first thing to do would be to learn what it is that makes the niches be niches. Where are there figurative mountains that you can't just run a road straight up and down, just because you can run it on either side and the mountain is simply a path that's halfway between? What makes a mule be infertile but a grolar bear not, and a horse-bear allograft chimera probably not viable at all? Why does ancient Greek verse about a "wine-dark sea" not connote that the sea is burgundy, the way it would in English?

I confess an attraction to what is apparently a "Faustian" view, that there's something like a right answer to questions of religion, and then one could be "safe" by finding it and running with it. But even if that were true, -- really, even the idea of that being "true" or "false" is perhaps kind of "Faustian" -- the way of finding it would have to pass through someone understanding the equivalents of those things.

replies.content: Yes, that sounds like Jupiter. The Tree of Life is among other things a chart of what balances what: Jupiter and Mercury balance each other, Mars and Venus balance each other, Saturn and Moon balance each other, and Sun and Earth balance each other. So if you're going to invoke Jupiter heavily, balance it with Mercury, which makes you less obsessive about control, and gives you things to talk about other than politics!

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Um, in what context? Current affairs? Electrical current? The current in a stream?

Because I'll be closing this down in two minutes, I'll guess that you mean the solar current and the telluric current. They're patterns on the inner planes, which many people perceive as a pair of flows (thus "currents"), one descending from the sun, the other rising from the center of the earth. What exactly they are is a matter for theoretical discussion, but they are certainly things that many people have experienced, and here as elsewhere, the experience is primary -- the theory is derivative.
